title: Apply: resources and population growth
description: Apply your understanding of resources and population growth in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below shows how the size of a certain population of frogs has changed over time. Three points are labeled on the graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Of the three points, which one shows where competition in the frog population was likely the highest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Competition tends to increase as the size of a population gets bigger.
So, competition is likely highest at the point where the population is the biggest.
Of the three points, the population is the biggest at point C.

options.alt: A graph is shown of the frog population size over time. The line on the graph is a curve resembling the left side of a hill, starting at the lower left. From left to right, the line curves upward slowly and then more steeply, and eventually levels off. Point A is about a quarter of the way up the upward curve. Point B is about halfway up the curve. Point C is at the top of the curve.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below shows how the size of a certain population of wolves has changed over time. Three points are labeled on the graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Of the three points, which one shows where competition in the wolf population was likely the lowest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Competition tends to increase as the size of a population gets bigger.
So, competition is likely lowest at the point where the population is the smallest.
Of the three points, the population is the smallest at point A.

options.alt: A graph is shown of the wolf population size over time. The line on the graph is a curve resembling the left side of a hill, starting at the lower left. From left to right, the line curves upward slowly and then more steeply, and eventually levels off. Point A is about halfway up the upward curve. Point B is about three-quarters of the way up the curve. Point C is at the top of the curve.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below shows how the size of a certain population of lionfish has changed over time. Three points are labeled on the graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Of the three points, which one shows where competition in the lionfish population was likely the highest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Competition tends to increase as the size of a population gets bigger.
So, competition is likely highest at the point where the population is the biggest.
Of the three points, the population is the biggest at point 3.

options.alt: A graph is shown of the lionfish population size over time. The line on the graph is a curve resembling the left side of a hill, starting at the lower left. From left to right, the line curves upward slowly and then more steeply, and eventually levels off. Point 1 is about a quarter of the way up the upward curve. Point 2 is about halfway up the curve. Point 3 is at the top of the curve.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of scientists measured the size of a bear population once a year for 6 years. Their data is shown in the graph below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement could explain the change in population size shown in the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A bar graph shows the size of a bear population over six years. The graph shows that in two thousand ten, there were about 225 bears. In two thousand eleven, there were about 250. In two thousand twelve, there were about 310. In two thousand thirteen, there were about 325. In two thousand fourteen, there were about 350. In two thousand fifteen, there were about 350.

choices.content: The amount of food available to the bear population increased between 2010 and 2015.
The number of hibernation sites available to the bear population decreased between 2010 and 2015.
The amount of water available to the bear population decreased between 2010 and 2015.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of scientists measured the size of a bird population once a year for 6 years. Their data is shown in the graph below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement could explain the change in population size shown in the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A bar graph shows the size of a bird population over six years. The graph shows that in two thousand ten, there were about 350 birds. In two thousand eleven, there were about 325. In two thousand twelve, there were about 305. In two thousand thirteen, there were about 250. In two thousand fourteen, there were about 225. In two thousand fifteen, there were about 225.

choices.content: The amount of food available to the bird population increased between 2010 and 2015.
The number of nesting sites available to the bird population decreased between 2010 and 2015.
The number of mating sites available to the bird population increased between 2010 and 2015.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of scientists measured the size of a grass plant population once a year for 6 years. Their data is shown in the graph below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement could explain the change in population size shown in the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A bar graph shows the number of grass plants in a population over a six year period. The graph shows that in two thousand ten, there were about 250 grass plants. In two thousand eleven, there were about 380. In two thousand twelve, there were about 500. In two thousand thirteen, there were about 760. In two thousand fourteen, there were about 850. In two thousand fifteen, there were about 850.

choices.content: The amount of sunlight available to the grass plant population decreased between 2010 and 2015.
The amount of water available to the grass plant population increased between 2010 and 2015.
The amount of soil nutrients available to the grass plant population decreased between 2010 and 2015.

assessment_items.question_content:
A scientist studied the size of three beetle populations. The scientist counted the number of beetles in each population once, and then again 12 months later. The data is shown in the table below.

Beetle population | Population size (month 1) | Population size (month 12)
- | - | -
1 | 25 beetles | 325 beetles
2 | 25 beetles | 285 beetles
3 | 25 beetles | 175 beetles

**Which beetle population likely had the most resources during the 12 months between counts?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

A scientist studied the size of three lizard populations. The scientist counted the number of lizards in each population once, and then again 12 months later. The data is shown in the table below.

Lizard population | Population size (month 1) | Population size (month 12)
- | - | -
1 | 30 lizards | 270 lizards
2 | 30 lizards | 450 lizards
3 | 30 lizards | 180 lizards

**Which lizard population likely had the most resources during the 12 months between counts?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

A scientist studied the size of three squirrel populations. The scientist counted the number of squirrels in each population once, and then again 12 months later. The data is shown in the table below.

Squirrel population | Population size (month 1) | Population size (month 12)
- | - | -
1 | 10 squirrels | 80 squirrels
2 | 10 squirrels | 60 squirrels
3 | 10 squirrels | 20 squirrels

**Which squirrel population likely had the fewest resources during the 12 months between counts?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The image below shows a woodland ecosystem in Northern California, in the United States.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following competitive interactions most likely occurs in the woodland ecosystem?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: "[Tilden Regional Park picnic area](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Tilden_Regional_Park_picnic_area.JPG)" by Ijon, [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/deed.en)

options.alt: A wide grassy field surrounded by trees.

choices.content: Coyotes compete for rabbits to eat.
Oak trees compete for carbon dioxide to use in photosynthesis.
Squirrels compete for oxygen to breathe.

assessment_items.question_content:
The image below shows a desert ecosystem in Utah, in the United States. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following competitive interactions most likely occurs in the desert ecosystem?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A large sandy area with dry plants and mountains in the background.

assessment_items.question_content:
The image below shows a stream ecosystem in England.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following competitive interactions most likely occurs in the stream ecosystem?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A stream of water lined by trees and other plants.

choices.content: Fish compete for insects to eat.
Trees compete for carbon dioxide to use in photosynthesis.
Frogs compete for oxygen to breathe.
